Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside And Or Vacate The Default Of Plaintiff Zenith Insurance Company; **Courtreporter Fees Paid**
Off Calendar Jul-14-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT WILLIAM WONG's Motion To Set Aside And Or Vacate The Default Of Plaintiff Zenith Insurance Company:

Defendant's motion to set aside default is granted.

Defendant's motion is brought on the ground that he was never served. (Mt., 2: 23-25; Wong Decl., 5.) Under section 473(d) of the Code of Civil Procedure, the court may upon motion "set aside any void judgment or order." "[C]ompliance with the statutory procedures for service of process is essential to establish personal jurisdiction. Thus, a default judgment entered against a defendant who was not served with a summons in the manner prescribed by statute is void. Under section 473, subdivision (d), the court may set aside a default judgment which is valid on its face, but void, as a matter of law, due to improper service." (Ellard v. Conway (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 540, 544; see Carol Gilbert, Inc. v. Haller (2009) 179 Cal.App.4th 852, 864-865 ["We question whether service that does not comply with statutory requirements should ever be allowed to stand, over a defendant's challenge, as the basis for a default judgment."].) Relief from a judgment that is void for improper service must be sought within 2 years of entry of the default judgment. (Rogers v. Silverman (1989) 216 Cal.App.3d 1114, 1126.)

Defendant declares that he was never personally served in this action - that instead, his son Allen Wong was served. (Wong Decl., 5-6.) Further, Defendant declares that he only became aware of this action when he received an Order to Show Cause on October 23, 2020. (Id. 8-9.) "It is the policy of the law to favor, whenever possible, a hearing on the merits . . . Therefore, when a party in default moves promptly to seek relief, very slight evidence is required to justify a trial court's order setting aside a default." (Shamblin v. Brattain (1988) 44 Cal.3d 474, 478.)

Plaintiff's argument that service of Allen Wong would comply with substituted service is rejected. To effect service by substituted service, the plaintiff must show that "a copy of the summons and complaint cannot with reasonable diligence be personally delivered to the person to be served." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 415.20(b).) "[T]he burden is upon the plaintiff to show reasonable diligence to effect personal service and each case must be judged upon its own facts. . . . We conclude that the substitute abode service on [defendant] was ineffective and void because [plaintiff] failed to comply with the mandatory prerequisite to such service by exercising reasonable diligence to effect personal service." (Evartt v. Superior Court (1979) 89 Cal.App.3d 795, 802.) Plaintiff provides no showing that Allen Wong was served only after it exercised reasonable diligence to effect personal service, or that it substantially complied with the statutory requirements of either personal service or substituted service. (See Carol Gilbert, 179 Cal.App.4th at 865-866 ["First, there must have been some degree of compliance with the offended statutory requirements."].)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)
